theorem_title: Finite-time blow-up for the semilinear heat equation by the energy method
theorem_statement:
Finite-time blow-up for the semilinear heat equation by the energy method.

Let \(\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^d\) be a bounded domain, and let \(u(t,x)\) be a sufficiently regular solution of
\[
u_t = \Delta u + |u|^{p-1}u
\qquad \text{in } \Omega,
\]
with homogeneous Dirichlet boundary condition
\[
u=0 \qquad \text{on } \partial\Omega,
\]
for some \(p>1\).

Define the energy
\[
E(t)
=
\frac12 \int_\Omega |\nabla u(t,x)|^2\,dx
-
\frac{1}{p+1}\int_\Omega |u(t,x)|^{p+1}\,dx.
\]

Assume the initial energy is nonpositive:
\[
E(0)\le 0.
\]

Then the quantity
\[
Y(t):=\int_\Omega |u(t,x)|^2\,dx
\]
satisfies a differential inequality that forces finite-time blow-up.
raw_proof_text:
We proceed in several steps.

First, differentiate the energy:
\[
\frac{dE}{dt}
=
\int_\Omega \nabla u \cdot \nabla u_t\,dx
-
\int_\Omega |u|^{p-1}u\,u_t\,dx.
\]
Integrate the first term by parts, using the homogeneous Dirichlet boundary condition so that the boundary term vanishes:
\[
\int_\Omega \nabla u \cdot \nabla u_t\,dx
=
-\int_\Omega (\Delta u)\,u_t\,dx.
\]
Therefore
\[
\frac{dE}{dt}
=
-\int_\Omega (\Delta u)\,u_t\,dx
-
\int_\Omega |u|^{p-1}u\,u_t\,dx
=
-\int_\Omega \bigl(\Delta u + |u|^{p-1}u\bigr)u_t\,dx.
\]
Using the equation
\[
u_t = \Delta u + |u|^{p-1}u,
\]
we obtain
\[
\frac{dE}{dt}
=
-\int_\Omega |u_t|^2\,dx \le 0.
\]
So the energy is nonincreasing:
\[
E(t)\le E(0)\le 0.
\]

Next define
\[
Y(t)=\int_\Omega |u|^2\,dx.
\]
Differentiate:
\[
\frac12 Y'(t)
=
\int_\Omega u\,u_t\,dx.
\]
Substitute the PDE:
\[
\frac12 Y'(t)
=
\int_\Omega u\Delta u\,dx
+
\int_\Omega |u|^{p+1}\,dx.
\]
Integrate the Laplacian term by parts, again using \(u=0\) on \(\partial\Omega\):
\[
\int_\Omega u\Delta u\,dx
=
-\int_\Omega |\nabla u|^2\,dx.
\]
So
\[
\frac12 Y'(t)
=
-\int_\Omega |\nabla u|^2\,dx
+
\int_\Omega |u|^{p+1}\,dx.
\]

Now rewrite this using the energy. By definition,
\[
E(t)
=
\frac12 \int_\Omega |\nabla u|^2\,dx
-
\frac{1}{p+1}\int_\Omega |u|^{p+1}\,dx.
\]
Multiplying by \(2\),
\[
2E(t)
=
\int_\Omega |\nabla u|^2\,dx
-
\frac{2}{p+1}\int_\Omega |u|^{p+1}\,dx.
\]
Rearranging gives
\[
-\int_\Omega |\nabla u|^2\,dx
+
\int_\Omega |u|^{p+1}\,dx
=
-2E(t)
+
\left(1-\frac{2}{p+1}\right)\int_\Omega |u|^{p+1}\,dx.
\]
Since
\[
1-\frac{2}{p+1}=\frac{p-1}{p+1}>0,
\]
we conclude
\[
\frac12 Y'(t)
=
-2E(t)
+
\frac{p-1}{p+1}\int_\Omega |u|^{p+1}\,dx.
\]
Because \(E(t)\le 0\), this implies
\[
\frac12 Y'(t)
\ge
\frac{p-1}{p+1}\int_\Omega |u|^{p+1}\,dx.
\]

Now apply Hölder’s inequality on the bounded domain \(\Omega\). Since \(p+1>2\),
\[
\int_\Omega |u|^{p+1}\,dx
\ge
C_\Omega \left(\int_\Omega |u|^2\,dx\right)^{\frac{p+1}{2}}
=
C_\Omega Y(t)^{\frac{p+1}{2}}
\]
for some constant \(C_\Omega>0\) depending only on \(\Omega\).

Therefore
\[
Y'(t)\ge c\,Y(t)^{\frac{p+1}{2}}
\]
for some constant \(c>0\).

Finally, since \(\frac{p+1}{2}>1\), the ODE
\[
z'(t)=c z(t)^{\frac{p+1}{2}}
\]
has solutions that blow up in finite time, and by comparison \(Y(t)\) cannot remain finite for all time. Thus the solution blows up in finite time.
